methods.description: This method investigates the miRPT model under two different perturbation approaches: perturbing initial aqueous-phase concentrations and perturbing spatial locations of immobile particles. The goal is to study the effects of these perturbations on the distribution and reaction speed of reactants.

steps.description: Study the effects of perturbing the initial aqueous-phase concentrations stored on the evenly-spaced immobile particles.
steps.searchable_summary: simulate perturbation of initial aqueous-phase concentrations in miRPT model

steps.description: Study perturbations of the spatial locations of the immobile particles.
steps.searchable_summary: simulate perturbation of spatial locations of immobile particles in miRPT model

steps.description: Conduct an ensemble of 100 realizations to accurately capture the statistics of the results.
steps.searchable_summary: perform ensemble simulations to capture statistical results

methods.description: This method uses a finite-difference model on a regularly-spaced grid, explicit in time, and second-order centered in space. It employs an operator-splitting approach between diffusion and reaction calculations.

steps.description: Choose a spatial grid with spatial step size ∆x = 1 cm, implying a number of cells, N C := L/∆x = 40.
steps.searchable_summary: set up spatial grid for finite-difference model

steps.description: Consider three different time step lengths of ∆t ∈ {259, 2592, 25920} s to calibrate the model.
steps.searchable_summary: calibrate finite-difference model with different time step lengths

methods.description: This method employs the miRPT algorithm for particle-tracking, which includes mass reduction, diffusive mass transfer, and fluid-solid interactions. It partitions total diffusion into random-walks and mass-transfers.

steps.description: Partition the total diffusion of the system into random-walks, mobile-to-immobile mass-transfers, and immobile-to-mobile mass-transfers.
Choose time step lengths to correspond to finite-difference simulations, considering ∆t ∈ {259, 2592, 25920} s.
steps.searchable_summary: align time step lengths with finite-difference simulations

steps.description: Hold the number of mobile particles (N M) to 4000 and vary the number of immobile particles (N I) for different cases.
